context: A reserved table should be ready within five minutes of the appointed time. This requires coordination between the host, bussers, and floor managers well before the guest arrives.
If a wait has been quoted, the guest should never wait more than ten minutes beyond that estimate. Exceeding this window signals a breakdown in floor management.
Once seated, the table should be acknowledged within one minute. This first interaction sets the tone and opens the opportunity for a beverage order.
Water should be offered and on the table within the first two minutes of seating. A choice of still, sparkling, or tap should always be presented before anything is poured.
The initial beverage order should be taken within three minutes of seating. This is the first service touchpoint that creates momentum for the meal.
All pre-dinner beverages, whether ordered at the table, the bar, or the lounge, should be served within seven minutes of being ordered. Delays here undermine the momentum of the entire experience.
Once a wine selection has been made, the bottle should be presented to the guest within three minutes. Label presentation and a tasting pour precede the full service.
When a guest's glass is empty, a refill or follow-up should be offered within thirty seconds. This is one of the most visible indicators of attentive service.
A check-back should occur within two minutes of the course being delivered. This confirms satisfaction and catches issues before they compound.
Finished plates should be cleared within three minutes. All guests at a table should be cleared together, never one at a time.
The dessert menu or dessert conversation should happen within three minutes of clearing the entree course. This maintains the flow and opens an additional revenue opportunity.
When a guest requests the check, it should be presented within two minutes. A slow check is one of the most common complaints in elevated dining.
Once payment has been collected, the processed receipt should be returned within three to five minutes. The departure experience should feel as smooth as the arrival.
Every table should receive a visual check or light touch every three to five minutes throughout the meal. The goal is consistent awareness without intrusion.
